contentBody: Vector databases are mainly designed for:
answerExplanation: Vector databases are specifically optimized for similarity-based retrieval using embeddings and vector operations.

contentBody: Which of the following are good examples of similarity search?
option.2: Finding images visually similar to a given photo
option.3: Finding documents similar in meaning to a user query
answerExplanation: Similarity search finds items that are semantically or visually related. Finding similar images and finding semantically similar documents are both classic similarity search tasks while exact ID lookups and range filters are not.

contentBody: Why are traditional databases not ideal for large-scale vector search?
option.4: They are not optimized for high-dimensional similarity search
answerExplanation: Traditional databases are designed for structured queries and exact matches. They lack the indexing and algorithms needed for high-dimensional vector similarity search.

contentBody: If two texts have similar meanings, their embeddings are usually:
option.3: Close to each other in vector space
answerExplanation: Embeddings are designed such that semantically similar texts are mapped to nearby points in vector space.
Semantic search retrieves results based on the meaning and context of a query rather than exact keyword matches.

contentBody: Which of the following queries are best handled by a vector database?
option.2: Find songs that sound similar to a given audio clip
option.3: Find articles similar to "how to scale microservices"
answerExplanation: Vector databases excel at semantic and perceptual similarity tasks. Finding similar songs and similar articles both require vector similarity search while exact ID lookups and date-based filters are handled better by traditional databases.

option.1: It requires no indexing at all
option.2: It is usually much faster than exact exhaustive search
option.3: It scales well to large high-dimensional datasets
option.4: It always returns worse results than exact search
answerExplanation: ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbor) search is faster than exhaustive exact search and is designed to scale efficiently to large high-dimensional datasets. It does use indexing structures and its accuracy is close to but not always exactly equal to exact search.

contentBody: Which of the following best describes similarity measurement conceptually?
option.1: Checking if two vectors have the same length
option.4: Measuring how close two vectors are in meaning
answerExplanation: Similarity measurement in vector databases involves computing distances or angles between vectors to determine how close they are in meaning.
